case_id: 150 MW coal-fired boiler three-element drum level control

pi_items.source: Matches task directory name and all script references
Matches task description, export_result.ps1 $projectPath, and setup_task.ps1 stale file deletion list
Matches setup_task.ps1 $projectsDir + filename, export_result.ps1 $projectPath, and task description

Three-element drum level control is a canonical power generation control problem. The narrative references a 150 MW coal-fired boiler. ASME PTC 4 and ISA-77 series are power generation standards. Confirmed via web search: ASME PTC 4 is 'Fired Steam Generators' performance test code, ISA-77 series covers fossil fuel power plant automation.

The task involves configuring an HMI/SCADA system (Red Lion Crimson 3.0) for industrial process control. This is the standard job function for controls engineers and SCADA automation engineers in power plants.

pi_items.metadata_value: ASME PTC 4 / ISA-77.44.01 / NFPA 85 / NERC CIP
pi_items.verified_value: ASME PTC 4 / ISA-77.42.01 (drum level) or ISA-77.44.01 (steam temp) / NFPA 85 / NERC CIP
pi_items.source: Web search confirmed: ASME PTC 4 is real (Fired Steam Generators). NFPA 85 is real (Boiler and Combustion Systems Hazards Code, covers alarms). NERC CIP is real (Critical Infrastructure Protection for power systems). ISA-77.44.01 is real but covers Steam Temperature Controls — the more precise standard for drum level control is ISA-77.42.01 (Feedwater Control System - Drum Type). However, the ISA-77 series as a whole covers fossil fuel power plant controls, and ISA-77.44.01 does address pressure compensation. The reference is within the correct standard family but not the exact sub-standard for drum level.

Cross-referenced against boiler_instrument_schedule.csv row 1 and narrative Section 4 alarm table. All values match exactly: Tag=LT_100, Type=Float, Unit=% level, Range 0-100, Alarms LL=25/L=35/H=65/HH=75.
Cross-referenced against CSV row 2 and narrative alarm table. All values match. MCR Feedwater Flow in narrative is 400 klb/hr, consistent with alarm_h=400.
Cross-referenced against CSV row 3 and narrative alarm table. All values match. MCR Steam Flow in narrative is 480 klb/hr, consistent with alarm_h=480.

Cross-referenced against CSV row 4 and narrative. Design Drum Pressure in narrative is 2000 psig (matches max_value). Normal Operating is 900 psig (used in formulas). Alarm values match narrative table.
Cross-referenced against CSV row 6. Source=Internal, no alarms. Control output range 0-100% matches the clamp logic in the control algorithm.

Narrative Section 2 states: 'FT_100_COMP = FT_100 * sqrt( PT_100 / 900 )'. CSV marks Source=Formula. The 900 divisor matches the Normal Operating Drum Pressure of 900 psig. The sqrt pressure compensation formula is standard engineering practice for orifice plate flow measurement (confirmed via web search of ABB and ISA references).
Narrative Section 2 states: 'FT_101_COMP = FT_101 * sqrt( PT_100 / 900 )'. Same compensation pattern applied to steam flow Annubar measurement.
Narrative Section 2 states: 'MASS_BAL = FT_100_COMP - FT_101_COMP'. This is the standard feedwater-steam mass balance calculation.
CSV row 5 shows TT_100 with Status=DECOMMISSIONED. It is the only decommissioned tag in the instrument schedule. The verifier.py checks for TT_100 in the tag export and caps score if found.
Narrative Section 3 states: 'Create a User Program named "DrumLevelControl"'. export_result.ps1 binary context search includes 'DrumLevelControl'.
Narrative Section 3 states: 'Assign to Background Task 1'. The metadata uses 'Background 1' which is the Crimson UI label for this setting.
Narrative Section 3 states: 'Tick rate: 100 ms'.
Narrative Section 5 states: 'Log Name: DrumLevelLog'. export_result.ps1 binary context search includes 'DrumLevelLog'.
Narrative Section 5 states: 'Log Interval: 1 second'. 1 second = 1000 milliseconds.
Narrative Section 5 states: 'Retention: 90 days minimum'.
Narrative Section 5 states: 'Tags to Log: LT_100, FT_100_COMP, FT_101_COMP, CV_100'. Exact match with metadata in both content and order.
Narrative Section 6 states: 'Page 1 Title: Boiler Drum'.

This is a verifier configuration parameter (not a data claim). It sets the tolerance for min/max range comparisons in programmatic verification. Consistent with the oilgas_flow_measurement_tags task which also uses 2.0% tolerance.

privileged_info_summary: The task requires configuring a three-element boiler drum level control system in Red Lion Crimson 3.0. Eight ACTIVE tags must be created (all Float type): LT_100 (Drum Level, 0-100 % level, alarms LL=25/L=35/H=65/HH=75), FT_100 (Feedwater Flow, 0-500 klb/hr, alarm H=400), FT_101 (Steam Flow, 0-600 klb/hr, alarm H=480), PT_100 (Drum Pressure, 0-2000 psig, alarms H=1800/HH=1900), CV_100 (Control Valve Output, 0-100 %, Internal source, no alarms). Three Formula tags: FT_100_COMP = FT_100 * sqrt(PT_100 / 900), FT_101_COMP = FT_101 * sqrt(PT_100 / 900), MASS_BAL = FT_100_COMP - FT_101_COMP. The DECOMMISSIONED tag TT_100 must NOT be configured. A User Program named 'DrumLevelControl' must be created on Background Task 1 at 100ms tick rate, implementing: Level_Error = LT_100 - 50.0; CV_100 = (Level_Error * 2.5) + (MASS_BAL * 0.8), clamped to 0.0-100.0. A data logger named 'DrumLevelLog' must record LT_100, FT_100_COMP, FT_101_COMP, CV_100 at 1-second (1000ms) intervals with 90-day retention. An operator display on Page 1 titled 'Boiler Drum' must include numeric indicators for LT_100, FT_100, FT_101, PT_100; a bar graph for CV_100; and a trend viewer for MASS_BAL. The project must be saved as boiler_drum_3element.c3 in C:\Users\Docker\Documents\CrimsonProjects\.
